generated_oml:
// C3: Physical sensing components
instance InternalTemperatureSensor1 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "High-precision DS18S20 1-Wire digital thermometer monitoring internal temperature of the incubator box. Measurement range: -55°C to +125°C with ±0.5°C accuracy. Connected via Raspberry Pi GPIO using 1-Wire bus protocol."
    DTDFVocab:producedData InternalTemperatureData1
]

instance InternalTemperatureSensor2 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "High-precision DS18S20 1-Wire digital thermometer monitoring internal temperature of the incubator box. Measurement range: -55°C to +125°C with ±0.5°C accuracy. Connected via Raspberry Pi GPIO using 1-Wire bus protocol."
    DTDFVocab:producedData InternalTemperatureData2
]

instance ExternalTemperatureSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "High-precision DS18S20 1-Wire digital thermometer monitoring ambient temperature outside the incubator box. Measurement range: -55°C to +125°C with ±0.5°C accuracy. Connected via Raspberry Pi GPIO using 1-Wire bus protocol."
    DTDFVocab:producedData ExternalTemperatureData
]

// C4: Physical-to-virtual interaction
instance InternalTemperatureData1 : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    base:desc "Periodic transmission of internal temperature data from InternalTemperatureSensor1 to RabbitMQ communication server for DT synchronization. Part of continuous polling mechanism by low-level driver."
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom InternalTemperatureSensor1
]

instance InternalTemperatureData2 : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    base:desc "Periodic transmission of internal temperature data from InternalTemperatureSensor2 to RabbitMQ communication server for DT synchronization. Part of continuous polling mechanism by low-level driver."
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom InternalTemperatureSensor2
]

instance ExternalTemperatureData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    base:desc "Periodic transmission of ambient temperature data from ExternalTemperatureSensor to RabbitMQ communication server for DT synchronization. Part of continuous polling mechanism by low-level driver."
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom ExternalTemperatureSensor
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance TwoParameterThermalModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Simplified differential equation-based thermal model with 2 free parameters for predicting internal temperature dynamics. Used for real-time control applications with lower computational requirements."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo PythonCalibrationEnabler, RabbitMQCommunicationEnabler
]

instance FourParameterThermalModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Advanced differential equation-based thermal model with 4 free parameters for accurate prediction of thermal inertia effects in the heatbed. Preferred for predictive analytics and high-fidelity simulations."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo PythonCalibrationEnabler, RabbitMQCommunicationEnabler
]

instance InternalTemperatureDataStream : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Real-time stream of internal temperature measurements from both internal sensors, aggregated for model calibration and monitoring purposes."
    DTDFVocab:fromData InternalTemperatureData1, InternalTemperatureData2
    DTDFVocab:inputTo PythonCalibrationEnabler, ModelSimulationEnabler
]

instance ExternalTemperatureDataStream : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Real-time stream of ambient temperature measurements used for environmental context in model predictions and fault detection."
    DTDFVocab:fromData ExternalTemperatureData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo PythonCalibrationEnabler, ModelSimulationEnabler
]

instance ActuatorFeedbackData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Operational state data from heatbed and fan actuators (power input, operational state) used to validate model predictions and detect actuator faults."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo PythonCalibrationEnabler
]

// C11: Enablers
instance RaspberryPiController : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Embedded controller running bang-bang control logic for temperature regulation. Manages sensor data acquisition, actuator commands, and communication with RabbitMQ server."
    DTDFVocab:enables DataAcquisitionService
]

instance PythonCalibrationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Python-based implementation of non-linear least squares calibration algorithms using SciPy. Adjusts model parameters in real-time using sensor data to minimize prediction errors."
    DTDFVocab:enables ModelCalibrationService, AnomalyDetectionService
]

instance RabbitMQCommunicationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Message broker facilitating asynchronous communication between Raspberry Pi, DT backend, and visualization tools. Ensures real-time data synchronization and decoupled system components."
    DTDFVocab:enables DataIntegrationService
]

instance ModelSimulationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Simulation environment executing differential equation-based thermal models to reproduce and predict system behavior under various conditions."
    DTDFVocab:enables BehavioralReproductionService, PredictiveAnalyticsService
]

// C6: Services
instance DataAcquisitionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Continuous collection of temperature data from all sensors via 1-Wire protocol and transmission to RabbitMQ server at periodic intervals."
    DTDFVocab:provides RealTimeTemperatureInsight
]

instance ModelCalibrationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Dynamic adjustment of model parameters using real-time sensor data to ensure alignment between DT predictions and PT behavior."
    DTDFVocab:provides ModelAccuracyInsight, CalibrationAdjustmentAction
]

instance AnomalyDetectionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Comparison of model predictions with real-time sensor data to detect deviations indicating potential faults in sensors or actuators."
    DTDFVocab:provides FaultDetectionInsight, AlertGenerationAction
]

instance BehavioralReproductionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Execution of calibrated thermal models to simulate current PT behavior including transient responses and thermal inertia effects."
    DTDFVocab:provides SystemBehaviorInsight
]

instance PredictiveAnalyticsService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Use of high-fidelity models to forecast system responses to hypothetical scenarios including environmental changes and actuator failures."
    DTDFVocab:provides PredictiveForecastInsight, OptimizationRecommendationAction
]

instance ControlOptimizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Integration with supervisory controllers to refine control strategies based on model predictions and real-time performance data."
    DTDFVocab:provides ControlEfficiencyInsight, ParameterAdjustmentAction
]

instance RealTimeMonitoringService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Continuous visualization of temperature data and system state with real-time alerts for safety violations or operational anomalies."
    DTDFVocab:provides OperationalStatusInsight, SafetyAlertAction
]

instance DataIntegrationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Integration of sensor data and model outputs into a unified data stream for real-time monitoring and analysis."
    DTDFVocab:provides OperationalStatusInsight
]

// C2: Acting Components
instance HeatBedActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Primary resistive heating element controlled via bang-bang logic to maintain internal temperature. Operates under Raspberry Pi control with no explicit safety constraints mentioned."
]

instance FanActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Secondary actuator providing uniform heat distribution within the incubator. Operates at 12V or 24V under Raspberry Pi control to enhance thermal uniformity."
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance RealTimeTemperatureInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Continuous stream of internal and external temperature measurements with timestamp information for operational awareness."
]

instance ModelAccuracyInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Quantitative measure of prediction error between calibrated models and actual sensor data, indicating model fidelity."
]

instance FaultDetectionInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Identification of anomalies in sensor data or actuator behavior that deviate from expected patterns, indicating potential faults."
]

instance SystemBehaviorInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Reproduction of current PT behavior including transient responses and thermal inertia effects as predicted by calibrated models."
]

instance PredictiveForecastInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Forecasted system responses to hypothetical scenarios including temperature stability under varying environmental conditions."
]

instance ControlEfficiencyInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Evaluation of current control strategy performance in terms of energy consumption, temperature stability, and response time."
]

instance OperationalStatusInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Real-time status of all system components including temperature readings, actuator states, and safety thresholds."
]

instance CalibrationAdjustmentAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automatic adjustment of model parameters using calibration algorithms to minimize prediction errors."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance AlertGenerationAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Triggering of alerts for operators when anomalies or safety violations are detected in the system."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance OptimizationRecommendationAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Suggested adjustments to control parameters or operational procedures based on predictive analytics."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic false
]

instance ParameterAdjustmentAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Modification of control parameters (e.g., heatbed power, fan speed) based on optimization insights."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance SafetyAlertAction : DTDFVocab:Action [

    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

// C1: Physical Components
instance InsulatedStyrofoamBox : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Thermal container minimizing heat losses with two internal temperature sensors and one external temperature sensor."
]

instance HeatBed : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Resistive heating element responsible for temperature regulation inside the incubator box."
]

instance IncubatorFan : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Fan providing uniform heat distribution within the enclosed space, operating at 12V or 24V."
]

instance RaspberryPiEmbeddedController : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Embedded system running control logic, sensor data acquisition, and communication protocols for the incubator CPS."
]

instance PhysicalIncubatorEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:desc "Closed-loop thermal control system operating in a cyber-physical environment with safety-critical temperature regulation requirements."
    base:contains InsulatedStyrofoamBox, HeatBed, IncubatorFan, RaspberryPiEmbeddedController
]

instance virtual_to_physical_interaction : DTDFVocab:VirtualToPhysical [
    base:desc "The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the incubator DT system is governed by a **bang-bang controller** with modifications, implemented on a **Raspberry Pi**. The controller receives **temperature measurements** from the PT via the **RabbitMQ server** and issues **control commands** to the **low-level driver**, which interacts directly with the PT’s actuators (fan and heatbed). The **command types** include:
  - **Heating activation** (turning on the heatbed for a duration **H**),
  - **Cooling activation** (implicitly via fan operation, though not explicitly detailed),
  - **Waiting periods** (duration **C**) to prevent overshooting the target temperature.
  The controller enforces **safety protocols** via the low-level driver, ensuring the PT remains in a safe state during operations. **Validation and authentication** mechanisms are not explicitly described, but the **safe protocol** in the low-level driver implies a form of state validation. **Feedback systems** are inherent to the controller’s logic, as it continuously monitors temperature deviations from the **lower limit (LL)** and **upper limit (UL)**. **Conflict resolution** is not explicitly addressed, but the **statechart-based control logic** (Figure 8) suggests deterministic handling of actuator commands. **Command execution confirmation** is implied through the **RabbitMQ server’s message acknowledgment** mechanism, though not explicitly detailed."
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system for the incubator case study operates with a temporal focus on **real-time synchronization** between the Physical Twin (PT) and its Digital Twin (DT) counterpart. The system relies on **periodic data collection** from temperature sensors (two internal and one external) to monitor the thermal state of the incubator. The **calibration algorithms** (e.g., Model A with two free parameters and Model B with four free parameters) are designed to update the DT models dynamically, with Model B specifically optimized for predicting the **thermal inertia of the heatbed**, which is critical for accurate temperature regulation. The DT framework includes **real-time visualization** of the incubator status, achieved by relaying controller and sensor data into a time-series database (implied but not explicitly detailed in the provided documents). The system also employs **Kalman filtering** for anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation, which requires low-latency processing of sensor data to correlate measured temperatures with unmeasured heatbed temperatures. While the exact synchronization frequency is not specified, the use of **co-simulations** (for what-if scenarios) suggests a need for **sub-second to millisecond-level granularity** in data processing, particularly for controller optimization and adaptive calibration. The self-adaptation loop (future work) implies a **dynamic time-scale adjustment** based on detected anomalies, where experiments are scheduled to gather new data for recalibration, potentially introducing variable latency depending on the experiment's duration and complexity."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system follows a **closed-loop cyber-physical architecture**, where the **Physical Twin (PT)**—comprising the insulated container, heatbed, fan, and temperature sensors—is continuously monitored and controlled by the **cyber layer** (Raspberry Pi controller and simulation models). The system is structured into three core layers: **Data Layer** (sensors collecting real-time temperature readings from the PT), **Model Layer** (Model A and Model B for thermal prediction), and **Algorithm Layer** (calibration algorithms to update model parameters based on sensor data). The **Data Layer** feeds sensor readings into the **Model Layer**, which generates predictions, while the **Algorithm Layer** ensures model accuracy through calibration. The architecture does not explicitly mention a **time-series database**, **Kalman filter**, or **co-simulation engines**, but the goals imply integration of these components for real-time visualization, anomaly detection, and adaptive control. The system is designed to **close the loop** by enabling dynamic adjustments to the PT based on DT insights, such as reconfiguring the controller or recalibrating models when anomalies (e.g., changes in thermal load) are detected."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle for the incubator case study includes the following phases: **1. System Definition and Requirements Gathering** – Establishing the goals (e.g., temperature regulation within an insulated box) and hardware/software specifications (e.g., Raspberry Pi controller, heatbed, sensors). **2. Model Development** – Creation of abstract representations (e.g., two simulation models with 2 and 4 free parameters, respectively) to capture thermal dynamics, including calibration methods to refine model accuracy. **3. Integration and Deployment** – Combining models, data streams (from sensors), and algorithms (e.g., fault detectors, state estimators) into a cohesive DT framework, as depicted in Figure 1, where calibration algorithms update models of the Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and environment. **4. Monitoring and Optimization** – Real-time data collection from sensors (e.g., two internal and one external temperature sensors) feeds into monitors to assess system performance, safety, and optimality. **5. Adaptation and Evolution** – Dynamic adjustments to the DT (e.g., reconfigurability for robustness) based on environmental changes or detected violations, with human or algorithmic intervention to modify the Physical Twin (PT). **6. Retirement/Archival** – Post-deployment, models and data are retained for future analysis, validation, or reuse, ensuring lifecycle governance through iterative refinement and documentation (e.g., publicly available repository for hardware/software specs)."
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "The fidelity of the incubator's Digital Twin models is governed by a **trade-off between complexity and computational efficiency**. The **two-parameter model** provides a simpler, faster simulation but lacks accuracy in predicting the **thermal inertia of the heatbed**. Conversely, the **four-parameter model** offers higher fidelity by better capturing the heatbed's dynamic behavior, albeit at the cost of increased computational complexity. Model validation is achieved through **non-linear least squares calibration**, where real-time sensor data is used to refine parameter estimates and ensure alignment with the Physical Twin's behavior. Uncertainty quantification is implicitly addressed by comparing the performance of the two models, with the **four-parameter model** selected for its superior predictive accuracy. Error detection is facilitated by **real-time monitoring** of sensor data against model predictions, enabling the DT to identify discrepancies and trigger corrective actions. However, the documents do not specify formal **quality assurance processes** such as benchmarking, failure mode analysis, or continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD) methodologies."
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "The **technical connection** infrastructure for the incubator DT system is built around a **message-broker architecture** using **RabbitMQ**, a lightweight, open-source message broker. The network topology follows a **star-like structure**, where the RabbitMQ server acts as the central node, facilitating communication between the **low-level driver**, **controller**, and **DT components**. The **communication protocol** is not explicitly named in the documents, but RabbitMQ’s default **Advanced Message Queuing Protocol (AMQP)** is implied as the underlying mechanism. The system does not specify **bandwidth or latency requirements**, but the lightweight nature of RabbitMQ and the periodic polling of sensor data suggest low-latency, high-frequency communication. **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are not explicitly detailed, though RabbitMQ’s native features (e.g., message persistence, acknowledgment) may contribute to robustness. **Security protocols** are not mentioned, but the use of a dedicated communication server implies isolation from external threats. The DT components are implemented as **listeners** to the messages exchanged between the controller and low-level driver, enabling real-time synchronization without direct physical connections."
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "The **hosting and deployment** infrastructure for the incubator DT system is **on-premises**, with all components running on a **Raspberry Pi 4 Model B**, a single-board computer. The **RabbitMQ server** is deployed locally on the Raspberry Pi, ensuring low-latency communication between the PT and DT. The system does not require **cloud or edge deployment**, as the computational and storage demands are minimal, aligning with the lightweight nature of RabbitMQ and the DT’s focus on real-time temperature regulation. **Scalability mechanisms** are not explicitly addressed, but the modular design (e.g., separate low-level driver, controller, and DT components) suggests potential for extension. **Availability and reliability** are assured through the **periodic polling** of sensor data and the **state-based control logic**, which prevents system instability. The deployment model is **monolithic** in nature, with all software components tightly integrated on the Raspberry Pi, though the use of RabbitMQ allows for **loose coupling** between modules. No explicit **high-availability or failover mechanisms** are described."
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin (DT) system for the incubator case study incorporates **safety-critical considerations** through its **monitoring and control mechanisms**, as depicted in Figure 1. The DT employs **calibration algorithms** to update models of the Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and its environment, ensuring accurate parameter estimation for safe operation. **Monitors** within the DT continuously check whether the system (both physical and cyber components) is performing **safely and optimally** under real-time conditions. When violations or deviations are detected, the system triggers **human or algorithmic interventions** to mitigate risks, ensuring fail-safe behavior. The DT framework inherently supports **predictability** by leveraging models to simulate and predict the behavior of the Physical Twin (PT) under current operating conditions, reducing the likelihood of unsafe states. However, explicit details on **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., encryption, access control, authentication systems), **threat mitigation strategies**, or **compliance with data protection regulations** (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA) are not provided in the documents. The system relies on a **Raspberry Pi** for control, but no specific security measures (e.g., network segmentation, intrusion detection) are mentioned."
]
